coredata.dc:title: Microplasma-enabled colloidal nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for broad-range fluorescent pH sensors
coredata.dc:creator: Kurniawan, Darwin
Chiang, Wei-Hung
coredata.dc:description: Here we report a liquid-phase, bottom-up synthesis of photoluminescent colloidal nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) under ambient conditions using atmospheric-pressure microplasmas. The nucleation, growth, and simultaneously N doping of GQDs were achieved using a single plasma-liquid process. The as-synthesized NGQDs showed a 6.39 nm averaged diameter with a narrow size distribution and room-temperature excitation-dependent photoluminescence with a quantum efficiency up to 30%. The stable colloidal NGQDs with polar nitrogen-doping configurations and oxygen-containing functional groups on surfaces exhibited protonation/deprotonation properties under the pH value ranging from 1.25 to 13.56 and can be used for broad-range pH detection. Our study not only provides a simple, scalable, and environmental-friendly method to synthesize NGQD under ambient condition but also the fundamental understanding of pH detection using NGQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dot
Nitrogen doping
Microplasma
Colloidal
Atmospheric-pressure

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Ministry of Science and Technology of Taiwan ( MOST Grant no. MOST 107-2628-E-011-002-MY3, MOST 107-2911-I-011-504 and MOST 108-2623-E-011-001-NU) and National Taiwan University of Science and Technology (NTUST). We acknowledged Professor Yu-Cheng Chiu and Livy Laysandra for the technical assistance of the rotary evaporator.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of NGQDs using microplasmas and absorbance and PL spectra of NGQDs.

(a) Schematics of experimental setup for NGQD synthesis using a microplasma reactor. (b) UV–visible absorbance and (c) PL spectra of NGQD synthesized at 9.6 mA fixed discharge current with different reaction time. (d) UV–visible absorbance and (e) PL spectra of NGQD synthesized at 60-min fixed reaction time with different discharge current. PL intensities were normalized for comparison in (c) and (e). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

ce:simple-para.ce:bold: OES study of NGQD synthesis using microplasmas.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a–c) Different sections of OES spectra of NGQDs synthesis with chitosan aqueous solution using microplasma at 9.6 mA discharge current. (d) Plot of normalized OH intensity with different reaction time. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

ce:simple-para.ce:bold: TEM characterization of NGQD synthesized by microplasmas.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images of NGQDs (inset: FFT image of NGQDs), (b) HRTEM image with lattice spacing of NGQDs, and (c) Corresponding histogram size distribution measured from TEM images. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

ce:simple-para.ce:bold: Characterizations of NGQDs synthesized by microplasmas.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) PL mapping, (b) XPS survey spectra, HRXPS spectra of (c) C1s, (d) N1s, and (e) O1s of the NGQDs. (f) MicroRaman spectra of chitosan and NGQDs. The NGQDs were synthesized by a microplasma at 9.6 mA discharge current for 60-min. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Photographs of 1.5 mg/mL NGQDs solutions in pH 1.78, 3.97, 7.09, 10.17, and 13.56 under white light and 365 nm UV irradiation. (b) PL spectra of 1.5 mg/mL NGQDs solutions with different pH values under 330 nm excitation. (c) Plot of the pH values versus PL intensity ratio of I/Io of NGQDs in different pH values under 330 nm excitation. (d) UV–visible absorption spectra of 1.5 mg/mL NGQDs solutions with different pH values (e) Plot of the pH values versus 320 nm absorbance ratio of A/Ao of NGQDs in different pH values. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

ce:simple-para.ce:bold: Study of protonation/deprotonation and pH response reversibility of NGQDs by PL spectroscopy and zeta potential measurement.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Maximum PL emission shifting behaviour under the corresponding maximum excitation wavelength (370, 380, 390, 390, and 420 nm for pH 1.78, 3.97, 7.09, 10.17, and 13.56, respectively) and (b) zeta potential of NGQDs in different pH value. (c) The pH response reversibility of 1.5 mg/mL NGQDs between pH 2.00 and 13.00. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Microplasma-enabled colloidal nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for broad-range fluorescent pH sensors

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, National Taiwan University of Science and Technology, Taipei, 10607, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Taiwan University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, National Taiwan University of Science and Technology, Taipei, 10607, Taiwan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here we report a liquid-phase, bottom-up synthesis of photoluminescent colloidal nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) under ambient conditions using atmospheric-pressure microplasmas. The nucleation, growth, and simultaneously N doping of GQDs were achieved using a single plasma-liquid process. The as-synthesized NGQDs showed a 6.39 nm averaged diameter with a narrow size distribution and room-temperature excitation-dependent photoluminescence with a quantum efficiency up to 30%. The stable colloidal NGQDs with polar nitrogen-doping configurations and oxygen-containing functional groups on surfaces exhibited protonation/deprotonation properties under the pH value ranging from 1.25 to 13.56 and can be used for broad-range pH detection. Our study not only provides a simple, scalable, and environmental-friendly method to synthesize NGQD under ambient condition but also the fundamental understanding of pH detection using NGQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) represent a unique zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials [1,2] and have attracted enormous attentions because of their exceptional properties including optical properties, tuneable bandgaps and surface functionalities, chemical and photostabilities, biocompatibility and nontoxicity [2,3], making them promising candidate for optoelectronics, energy generation and storage, catalysis, and biomedical applications [4,5]. Among those applications, pH sensing using GQDs is especially attractive and important because pH value plays a critical role in chemical, biological, and environmental systems. A small variation in pH value not only affects the environmental stability and safety, such as drinking water [6], but also biological systems to induce physiological dysfunction [7], wound infection [8,9], and cancer or tumor cells generation [10,11].

Doping GQDs with heteroatoms such as nitrogen (N) and/or Boron (B) is an effective approach to change the properties of GQDs. Recently N-doped GQDs (NGQDs) with improved photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY), tuneable surface polarities, strong electron withdrawing ability, and negligible environmental and biological hazards [12–16] have been shown useful for a wide range of applications including chemical and biological sensing [17–20], bioimaging [21–23], drug delivery [24], catalysis [25,26], and renewable energy generation [1,27]. NGQDs have been demonstrated as promising fluorescent pH sensors with superior sensitivity, selectivity, rapidity, and applicability for biological and medical applications [6,28,29], compared with conventional short-lived organic fluorescent dyes, toxic semiconductor quantum dots, and expensive noble-metal nanoparticles [30]. Previous studies reported that the PL intensities of GQDs were significantly changed when the pH was changed due to protonation and deprotonation of surface functional groups, suggesting that GQDs are optically sensitive to pH and bearing a great potential for pH sensing [6,29,31–33].

Generally, NGQDs synthesis methods are classified into two major types including direct synthesis using top-down or bottom-up technologies and postgrowth doping [21]. Direct synthesis using top-down technology usually involves chemical or mechanical cutting from pre-synthesized N-doped carbon materials such as graphene, carbon nanotubes, and carbon fibers into NGQDs via chemical oxidation, electrochemical dissociation, and physical exfoliation. The bottom-up methods normally utilize carbon- and nitrogen-containing molecules to generate NGQDs through carbonization, pyrolysis or step-wise chemical reaction routes using wet chemical or plasma methods [1–3,31,34]. While large scaled production is possible using top-down approach, it is challenging to synthesize NGQDs with controlled structures. Moreover, it is necessary to use large amount of strong acids and toxic chemicals [15]. On the other hand, it is possible to synthesize GQDs with controlled properties using bottom-up approach. However, special precursors and chemicals [12], long reaction time, and laborious preparation procedures are required [13–15,17]. Synthesis of NGQDs by postgrowth doping usually requires a long reaction time to treat the mixtures of nitrogen precursors and pre-synthesized GQDs under high temperature [17]. Overall, it is still lacking a simple, scalable, and environmental-friendly method to synthesize NGQDs with well-controlled structures.

Here we report a direct synthesis of NGQDs under ambient conditions using chitosan as the sole carbon and nitrogen precursor by atmospheric-pressure microplasmas. During the synthesis, no strong acid or toxic chemicals were additionally used. The synthesized NGQDs were further used for facile and sensitive pH detection with a broad range (pH 1.25 to 13.56). Microplasmas are unique type of discharge with one dimension less than 1 mm and recently have been shown to be useful for nanomaterials synthesis and processing in both the gas and liquid phases. In particular, the property of high electron density (∼10 eV) allows the non-thermal dissociation of molecular gases to form high concentrations of reactive radical species, leading the synthesis of metal nanoparticles and GQDs in the electrolyte solutions [35–39]. Detailed microscopic and spectroscopic characterizations indicates NGQDs with 6.39 nm mean diameter with narrow size distribution (16% standard deviation) can be synthesized directly by interacting an argon (Ar) microplasma with chitosan aqueous solution at ambient conditions. Moreover, the N atoms were doped into the sp2 carbon lattice of GQDs during the plasma synthesis. The synthesized colloidal NGQDs show stable dispersion and photoluminescence (PL) properties with the 525 nm maximum emission under 460 nm excitation, leading dual optical responses to the pH monitoring using PL and absorbance spectroscopies. PL spectroscopic study showed a broad linear range from pH 1.78 to 13.56 and absorption spectroscopic measurement provided two linear ranges from pH 1.25–10.17 and pH 10.17–13.24, showing a potential of pH detection for almost the entire range. It is noted that the NGQDs surface polarities induced by the N-dopants play a critical role for the protonation during the pH detection. Our work not only provides a simple, scalable, and environmental-friendly method to synthesize NGQDs under ambient condition but also the fundamental understanding of pH detection using NGQDs for emerging biomedical applications including intracellular pH sensing [6,40], cancer or tumor cell detection [29], and wound-status monitoring [28].

Chitosan (low molecular weight, averaged 120,000 Da, CAS: 9012-76-4), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, ≥98%, CAS: 1310-73-2) pellets were purchased from Sigma Aldrich Co. Ltd. Acetic acid glacial (CH3COOH, ≥99.7%, CAS: 64-19-7) was purchased from Fischer Scientific. Hydrochloric acid (HCl, reagent grade, 37%, CAS: 7647-01-0) was purchased from Scharlau. Pt foil (99.95%, 20 mm
×
20 mm
×
0.1 mm) was purchased from Guv Team International Co. Ltd. Taiwan.

The colloidal NGQDs were synthesized in aqueous solutions using a direct current (dc) Ar microplasma electrochemical reactor operated at ambient conditions. The experimental setup is schematically shown in Fig. 1 a and details can be found in the experimental section and our previous report [39]. Briefly, precursor solution containing 0.03 mM chitosan dissolved in 20 mL of different concentrations of acetic acid (1.4 M, 1 M, 0.6 M and 0.1 M) and diluted two times by DI water. 10 mL of the obtained chitosan solution was then subjected to microplasma treatment [35,39] (Fig. 1a) under different plasma current (5–9.6 mA) and reaction time (10–80 min) to synthesize NGQDs.

The as-synthesized NGQDs solution was neutralized by adding 2 M NaOH solution. Then, acetone with volume ratio of 0.5–1 of that NGQDs solution was added to precipitate the unreacted chitosan. The suspension was filtered using vacuum filtration to get a mixture of NGQDs solution with acetone. The obtained solution was evaporated using rotary evaporator. Once all the solution had been evaporated, a little amount of methanol was added to redisperse the NGQDs stuck to the sample vial wall. Finally, the suspension was dried once again using rotary evaporator and fine NGQDs powder was obtained.

PL measurements of all liquid NGQDs dispersions were performed at room temperature using a commercial spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon Nanolog-3 spectrofluorometer) equipped with an InGaAs NIR detector and a 20 nm bandpass for both emission and excitation. The PL spectra obtained were scaled according to the measured excitation power. Absorbance spectra of all materials were recorded using a JASCO V676 absorbance spectrophotometer with a matched pair of 1 cm path length quartz cuvettes. The corresponding solvents were used as baseline reference for every spectral measurement. MicroRaman measurement was conducted at room temperature with a JASCO 5100 spectrometer (laser excitation wavelength λ = 532 nm). The purified NGQDs and chitosan samples were drop-casted on Si wafers and dried on a hotplate at 35
°C
in ambient condition. The laser power was adjusted and maintained at 0.1 mW to avoid sample damages by the laser. Silicon wafer was used to calibrate A silicon wafer with a Raman shifts of 520 cm−1 peak was used to calibrated the Raman spectrometer before the measurement. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed using an ESCALAB Xi+ (Thermo Fisher Scientific, United Kingdom) with monochromatic Al Kα X-ray radiation as the source gun type, a pass energy of 150.0 eV, and a beam size of 650 μm. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were performed using a field emission gun TEM (FEI Tecnai™ G2 F-20 S-TWIN) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. TEM samples were prepared from solution dry-casting of the NGQDs colloidal solution onto formvar/carbon-coated copper grids (300 mesh, EM Resolutions Ltd.). Digital Micrograph 3.7 software was utilized to obtain the particle size distribution. Zeta potential measurement was carried out using a NanoBrookZetaPlus (Brookhaven Instruments Co. United States). Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra was recorded in solution phase using FTIR-iS10 (NicoletTM) at room temperature. Background correction was conducted prior to sample measurement.

Different pH value solutions containing 0.1 M phosphate buffer saline (PBS) were prepared by adjusting the pH value with 2 M HCl and 2 M NaOH. NGQDs powder was added to the aforementioned solutions with the concentration of 1.5 mg/mL. After sitting for 5 min, the PL intensity of the NGQDs dispersion with varying pH values was measured at different excitation wavelength.

UV–Visible absorption spectroscopy was used to identify the optical absorption of various electronic states present in the synthesized NGQDs. Fig. 1b shows the UV–Visible absorbance spectra of the samples synthesized with 9.6 mA current and different reaction time. The broad absorption bands shown suggest the existence of various electronic states in the as-synthesized NGQDs [39]. The highest absorption of each spectrum at 281 nm is attributed to π
→
π∗ transitions of CC structures [41] and may also due to n
→
π∗ transitions of C–N bonds [42]. Moreover the absorption band ranging from 300 to 400 nm is attributed to n
→
π∗ transitions of CO or C–O bonds [39,41]. PL spectroscopy was performed to investigate the optical emission of NGQDs, in which both maximum excitation and emission are strongly correlated with particle sizes. Red-shifting behaviours will be observed when the NGQDs become bigger due to lower bandgaps. Fig. 1c shows the normalized PL spectra of the samples synthesized with 9.6 mA current and different reaction time under the corresponding maximum excitation (360, 400, 430, 460, 460 nm). The sample synthesized with 10 min exhibited a maximum PL emission peak at 441 nm, and the maximum PL peak was gradually shifted to 479, 506, 528 and 530 nm for the samples synthesized with 20, 40, 60 and 80 min, respectively. The growth of NGQDs can be observed from the red-shifted time while the absorption peak is still increasing (Fig. 1b), suggesting that 60-min reaction time may be the critical time for the emission in the PL spectra with increased reaction time (Fig. 1c). It is noted that the emission stopped red-shifting after 60-min reaction NGQDs growth in our synthesis condition. The 60-min reaction time was selected in the work to study the effect of plasma current. It was observed that the higher the plasma current the higher the absorbance of the absorbance peaks (Fig. 1d) and the red-shifting of the PL peaks (Fig. 1e) would be, suggesting production yields and sizes of the NGQDs are increased with increased plasma currents.

To study the plasma chemistry, in situ optical emission spectroscopy (OES) was performed. As shown in Fig. 2 a–c, several emission bands of the excited species can be observed, including molecular NO bands below 273 nm, OH (A
→
X) at 306–309 nm, N2 second positive system (C
→
B) at 280–450 nm, a weak Hα line between N2 first positive system (B
→
A) around 655.3 nm, atomic O transition (2s22p33s-2s2sp33p) around 776.3 nm, and Ar lines between 690 nm and 850 nm [39,43,44]. The observed reactive radicals and solvated electrons (eaq −) formed during the plasma treatment can play a role to generate smaller hydrocarbon moieties by cleavaging the β-1-4-glycosidic linkages of chitosan [45]. The decreasing of OH line at 309 nm with increased time shown in Fig. 2d suggests that OH radicals can be the key species for NGQDs synthesis. Generally, the synthesis of NGQDs are initiated by the reactive species including solvated electrons and OH radicals generated during the microplasma liquid process. Solvated electrons can be used to produce OH radicals via water dissociation (
H 2
O
+
e −
→ H + O H +
e −
) [44]. The generation of solvated electrons by plasmas can be increased with the increasing plasma current. Therefore, both the concentrations of solvated electrons and OH radicals are increased by increasing the applied plasma current. Consequently, the yield of NGQDs can be enhanced by increased the applied plasma current (Fig. 1d). In the recent report from our group, GQDs synthesis from an organosulfate, we proposed that radicalization of precursor molecules would occur after the fragmentation process, followed by cyclization and reconstruction to form GQDs [39]. This idea leads us to believe that similar reaction pathways may also happen for NGQDs synthesis from chitosan.

TEM was performed to study the morphologies of the synthesized NGQDs. The TEM image presented in Fig. 3 a shows particle-like nanostructures with no aggregation and amorphous coating observed. The fast Fourier transform (FFT) image in the inset reveals the crystalline structure of NGQDs. The lattice spacing of 0.24 nm can be confirmed through high resolution TEM (HRTEM), indicating (1120) lattice fringes of graphene [46] (Fig. 3b). The corresponding size distribution histogram shown in Fig. 3c indicates the 6.39 ± 1.01 nm average particles size based on a normal distribution fitting by taking 60 different particles.

The NGQDs synthesized at 60-min reaction time and 9.6 mA current exhibit an excitation-dependent emission with the maximum emission at 532 nm excited at 460 nm (Fig. 4 a). XPS was used to study the existence of nitrogen doping and functional groups on NGQDs. Fig. 4b shows the XPS spectrum showing C1s, N1s, and O1s peaks at 284.4, 399.4, and 531.8 eV, respectively. Sodium (Na) was observed due to the neutralization with NaOH during the purification. High-resolution XPS (HRXPS) was additionally performed to study the bonds configurations. The high resolution C1s peak indicates several peaks, consisting of CC, C–C, C–N, C–O, CN, and COOH at 284.4, 284.8, 285.6, 286.2, 287.4, and 288.1 eV [6,29,40,47–49], respectively (Fig. 4c). Moreover, high resolution N1s peak can be deconvoluted into pyridinic N (398.5 eV) [15], amino N (399.5 eV) [47,50], pyrrolic N (399.6 eV) [48], and graphitic N (401.0 eV) [15], as shown in Fig. 4d. Peak shown at 406 eV could be due to the nitrogen-related species absorbed on the GQDs surface since the synthesis was carried out at ambient condition [51]. Furthermore, the high resolution O1s peak (Fig. 4e) exhibits four peaks at 530.9, 532.1 [40], 533.2 [52] and 535.3 eV, which can be assigned to C–OH, OC–OH, C–O–C, and Na KLL Auger respectively. The XPS analysis suggests that the as-synthesized NGQDs contain nitrogen dopants and are rich with nitrogen- and oxygen-containing surface functional groups. XPS spectra of chitosan precursor is shown in Figure S1, and Table S1 summarizes the bonds percentage of chitosan precursor and as-synthesized NGQDs. It is noted that the increased percentage of sp2 carbon bonds in NGQDs can be attributed to the change of amorphous chitosan structure into crystalline graphene structure. In addition, the nitrogen bonds in chitosan are in the form of functional groups of –NH2 and –N–C due to the incomplete deacetylation of chitin, while the recombination of fragmented chitosan molecules during the plasma treatment leads to the formation of different types of nitrogen bonds including pyrrolic N as the highest (59.62%), then followed by pyridinic N (23.43%), and graphitic N (9.43%), schematically illustrated in Figure S2. FTIR measurement was additionally performed to confirm the functional groups of NGQDs, and the result is provided in Figure S3. The two strong bands observed at 3511 and 3167 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibrations of hydroxyl (−OH) and amine (N–H) groups, respectively [24,53]. Other oxygen containing functional groups including carboxylic (−COOH) and C–O–C groups are confirmed from the occurrence of CO and C–O stretching vibrations from –COOH group at 1559 and 1076 cm−1, respectively [48,53] and C–O–C stretching vibration at 1152 cm−1 [53]. Moreover, two peaks located at 2924 and 2853 cm−1 can be ascribed to C–H stretching vibrations belong to the benzene ring of NGQDs [54,55]. The existence of N-doping is supported from the peak occurrence at 1624 cm−1, stretching vibration of pyridinic structure (CN) [54,55], overlapped with CC vibrational stretching of benzene ring [48]. C−NH bending vibration located at 1416 cm−1 also indicates that nitrogen atom has been successfully doped into the GQDs [53]. In addition, the peaks observed between 675 and 835 cm−1 belong to C–H bending vibrations of deformed benzene rings due to non-graphitic N-doping [55]. Notably, the FTIR and the XPS results are consistent and suggest the existence of N-doped graphene-like structure functionalized with oxygen rich and amine functional groups which are crucial for the stability and pH sensing application in aqueous system.

MicroRaman characterization was performed to study the vibrational structures of chitosan and NGQDs (Fig. 4f). Chitosan shows a broad G-band at 1571 cm−1 due to its amorphous structures [50], while the NGQDs exhibit not only a sharp G-band at 1598 cm−1, referring to high crystalline graphene structure [56], but also the D-band of NGQDs located at 1348 cm−1 originated from structural disorder in the graphene lattice caused by N-dopant, vacancy due to non-graphitic N-doping, and edges [51]. As a consequence, the averaged intensity ratio of the D-band and G-band (ID/IG) was calculated to be 1.02 ± 0.06, indicating the disordered carbon atom and yet highly crystalline. This result is close to what has been reported by Kumar et al. [15], which further confirms that the NGQDs contain a large number of defects in the form of edges and surface functional groups. Moreover, NGQDs show no observable Raman peak above 1600 cm−1, unlike chitosan. This result indicates that the purification is successful and can increase the practicability of NGQDs, especially for intracellular pH sensing [29]. Overall, the above results suggest that our method provides a simple and environmental-friendly method to synthesize photoluminescent NGQDs under ambient conditions using microplasmas, comparable with previous reports [15,50,57,58].

The excitation-dependent emission property of the as-synthesized NGQDs (Fig. 4a) leads us to exploit them as pH sensors for sensing pH value with a broad range. The NGQDs synthesized by a microplasma at 9.6 mA discharge current for 60-min were used to study the pH section. First, a PL shifting accompanied with PL intensity changes of NGQDs under different pH in solutions can be directly observed by PL mapping (Figure S4) and white light photographs (Fig. 5 a). The PL emissions of NGQDs were blue-shifted in acidic condition and simultaneously provided strong blue light emission under 365 nm UV light irradiation. In contrast, when the pH became more alkaline, red-shifted behaviour as well as weaker emission were observed (Fig. 5a). Fig. 5b shows the PL spectra of NGQDs in different pH values, which are excited at the optimum sensing performance of 330 nm excitation wavelength. The optimum excitation wavelength used for pH sensing was investigated by extracting the maximum emission intensities for each excitation wavelengths obtained from the PL mapping results shown in Figure S4. The sensitivity was reflected by the slope obtained from the linear line fitting while the linearity was based on the r-squared (R2) value. The study indicates that the 330 nm excitation wavelength exhibits the most sensitive and linear for pH sensing, as shown in Figure S5 (see supporting information S5 for more details). Taking the original PBS buffer prior to pH alteration (pH 7.54) as the reference, the emission intensity of NGQDs was found to be quenched in more alkaline condition up to pH 13.56, and hence showing the “turn-off” state of NGQDs. The “turn-on” state, on the other hand, occurred in a more acidic condition until the pH reached 1.78. For pH values below 1.78, the emission intensity was observed to be reduced, indicating the limit of detection (LoD) of pH sensing using the as-synthesized NGQDs. An experimental linear correlation was established by plotting the pH values from 1.78 to 13.56 versus PL intensity ratio of I/Io under 330 nm excitation, as shown in Fig. 5c, where I and I0 refer to the PL intensity of NGQDs in aqueous solution with varying pH values and initial PL intensity of NGQDs in 0.1 M PBS aqueous solution, respectively under 330 nm excitation. It is significant that the correlation shows a high linearity with the high R2 value of 0.997 over the broad range of pH values from 1.78 to 13.56, suggesting the potential of the as-synthesized NGQDs for pH sensing.

Moreover, the as-synthesized NGQDs provide another sensing method using UV–Visible absorption spectroscopy. Higher absorbance of NGQD absorption peak at 281 nm (Fig. 1b) was observed for NGQDs in a more alkaline condition while lower absorbance of NGQDs absorption peak was detected for NGQDs in a more acidic condition, depicted in Fig. 5d. Experimental linear correlations were further analysed by plotting the pH values from 1.25 to 13.24 versus absorbance ratio of A/Ao, as shown in Fig. 5e, where A and A0 refer to the absorbance at 320 nm of NGQDs in aqueous solution with varying pH values and initial absorbance at 320 nm of NGQDs in 0.1 M PBS aqueous solution, respectively. It requires two linear correlations to be used to present the pH sensing with a range from 1.25 to 13.24. As described in Fig. 5e, the first linear correlation shows good linearity with a R2 value of 0.992 in the range of pH 1.25–10.17, and then followed by the second correlation for pH 10.17–13.24 with a R2 value of 0.997. The PL-based and absorption-based methods can be used together to complement each other to cover almost the entire pH range (1.25–13.56). In addition, the utilization of two methods can give a more confident result during the pH sensing. Overall, our work presents the first report of using NGQDs for pH sensing with a very broad range by PL and absorption spectroscopic methods (see supporting information Table S2) [6,28–30,32,40,42,47,48,53,59].

Previous study suggested that the origin of pH sensing could be due to the electron donating and withdrawing between the protons and surface functional groups on the GQDs [6,32,56,60,61]. In addition, Zhu et al. reported that the presence of graphitic N-doping in the carbon lattice provided a strong negative induction effect that could alter the energy level due to the change of electron density in the GQDs [55]. Nonetheless, it is important to note that the origin of GQDs PL mechanism still remains unclear and further investigation is needed. Thereby, we propose the successful utilization of NGQDs as pH sensor can be attributed to the coexistence of surface functional groups and N-doping in the carbon lattice. According to the XPS and FTIR results (Fig. 4c–e & Figure S3), as-synthesized NGQDs contain nitrogen- and oxygen-containing groups, especially amine, hydroxyl (−OH) and carboxylic acid groups (−COOH). Generally, the nitrogen in amine contains lone pair electrons which can be readily donated to the GQDs [56]. The amine groups can be deprotonated to form –NH2 group under alkaline condition, making the lone pair electrons to be donated to the GQDs [62]. At the same time, deprotonations of hydroxyl and carboxylic acid groups also happen [47], leading the formation of –O− and –COO− that can weaken the electron transfer from GQDs to the surface functional groups. As a result, the electron density in the GQDs is increased and simultaneously lowers the bandgap, providing the red-shifting of maximum PL emission (Fig. 6 a) and quenching of PL intensity (Fig. 5a). Choudhury et al. also reported that the deprotonation of –OH groups at high pH value creates a new surface state with lower bandgap responsible for the red-shifted emission of GQDs [60]. In contrast, the amine functional group will be protonated (−NH3 +) in acidic condition. Hence, the positively charged amine will neutralize the lone electrons pair and dismiss the electron transfer to GQDs. The strong electronegativity of N atom in –NH3 + can lessen the electron density in the GQDs, which gives result to blue-shifted maximum PL emission and increased PL intensity [56], as described in Figs. 6a and 5a respectively. The protonation and deprotonation of amine groups are further supported with the FTIR spectra in Figure S6, where the NH2 peak decreases and a new peak around 3100 cm−1 which belongs to NH3 + occurs. Previous study suggests that strong PL intensity exhibited by GQDs at low pH value is attributed to strong vibrational coupling of –OH produced by hydrogen bonding effect with the surrounding medium. This can cause energy level broadening and improve the conformational rigidity of GQDs. However, hydrogen bonding effect is eliminated at high pH value and gives result to reduced vibrational coupling and more discrete energy level, leading to blue-shifted PL emission with reduced intensity [60]. Thus, by taking advantage of this PL spectral shifting and intensities changes, the 330 nm excitation wavelength can be exploited as the optimum excitation wavelength for pH sensing, as described Fig. 5c.

The presence of nitrogen doping in the carbon lattice contributes to the existence of n-orbital and the electrons are localized within this orbital instead of π-orbital due to the strong negative induction effect [55,63]. The n-orbital will serve as the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the PL should be originating from n
→
π∗ excitation of the N state, which is consistent with previous reported works [54,64,65]. However, the negative induction effect of these aromatic doped N atoms are affected by pH value [55,66]. Gradual protonation occurs at acidic condition and gives result to weaker negative induction effect for graphitic N [55] and especially pyridinic N which possesses low pK a value around 4.6 [66]. On the contrary, the unprotonated aromatic N doping in alkaline condition possesses strong negative induction effect due to high electronegativity of N atom [55], assisting the energy level narrowing due to electron transfer. As such, it can be concluded that as the pH value becomes higher, the HOMO level originating from the N-state increases, and new surface state originating from the deprotonation of –OH occurs. The above hypothesis is supported by the red-shifted of both maximum excitation and emission at higher pH value (Fig. 6a).

In the aspect of pH sensing based on UV–Visible absorption, the possible mechanism is due to the ionization of hydroxyl and carboxylic acid groups with increasing pH value. To validate the above hypothesis, zeta potential measurement of NGQDs in five different pH value of PBS buffer solution (pH 2.14, 4.42, 7.00, 10.40, and 12.77) were conducted and the result is shown in Fig. 6b. Interestingly, the zeta potential data indicates a decreased linearly with increasing pH value. This result clearly proves the deprotonation of amine group and ionization of hydroxyl and carboxylic acid groups in higher pH value, which is in consistence with previous reports [32,62]. Therefore, the suggested sensing mechanism seems very plausible as both 330 and 320 nm used for PL and UV sensing respectively are within the absorption band of n
→
π∗ transitions of C–N, C–O, and CO bonds of NGQDs (see Fig. 1b).

The reversibility of PL intensity changes in very low and high pH value was also investigated, as shown in Fig. 6c. The result shows that pH sensing using NGQDs are quite reversible until seventh cycle and the response can be easily observed by naked eye (Fig. 6c inset). Light yellow solution color under daylight and bright luminescence under 365 nm UV lamp are exhibited with NGQDs in low pH value while darker yellow solution color and dimmed luminescence in high pH value. The remarkable sensing performance can also be contributed by the quantum yield of NGQDs that reaches up to ∼30% using rhodamine 6G (R6G) as reference. [67] To be more feasible in the pH sensing application, the photostability of NGQDs in high ionic strength solution was also investigated. The as-produced NGQDs were very stable even in high ionic strength solution (0.5–2 M NaCl solution), as depicted in Figure S7, where the emission intensity remains almost constant. This result further indicates that Na+ and Cl− ions do not affect the fluorescence properties of NGQDs, in which it will be beneficial in the practicability of NGQDs in a salt containing media. Moreover, the PL properties of either GQDs or NGQDs has been reported to remain stable after being exposed continuously to 365 nm UV irradiation [20], demonstrating an excellent photostability which further amplifies the applicability of NGQDs. It is also important to note that either GQDs or NGQDs possess low cellular toxicity or good biocompatibility [20,53,68], bearing a great potential for in vitro or even in vivo pH sensing and biological imaging.

Analytical PL and UV–Vis absorbance based pH detection of real water samples were performed for demonstrating the feasibility of the synthesized NGQDs as pH sensor. Table S3 depicts the analytical measurement comparison from a Clean pH500 pH meter, ratiometric PL intensity, and UV–Vis absorbance methods. It can be observed that the results are reliable which indicate that the synthesized NGQDs are highly applicable.

In summary, we have demonstrated a liquid-phase, bottom-up synthesis of photoluminescent colloidal NGQDs under ambient conditions using chitosan as the sole carbon and nitrogen precursor by atmospheric-pressure microplasmas for broad-range pH detection. The synthesized NGQDs can be used for facile and sensitive pH detection with a broad range (pH 1.25 to 13.56) using PL and UV–Visible absorbance spectroscopies. Detailed characterizations indicate NGQDs with narrow size distribution can be synthesized directly by interacting an Ar microplasma with chitosan aqueous solution at ambient conditions. The synthesized colloidal NGQDs show stable dispersion and PL properties, and the N dopants and surface functional groups on NGQDs surfaces play a critical role for the protonation/deprotonation during the pH detection. Our work not only provides a simple, scalable, and environmental-friendly method to synthesize NGQDs under ambient condition but also the fundamental understanding of pH detection using NGQDs for emerging biomedical, environmental, energy and catalysis applications.
Darwin Kurniawan: Formal analysis. Wei-Hung Chiang: Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

MOST 107-2628-E-011-002-MY3, MOST 107-2911-I-011-504 and MOST 108-2623-E-011-001-NU) and National Taiwan University of Science and Technology (NTUST). We acknowledged Professor Yu-Cheng Chiu and Livy Laysandra for the technical assistance of the rotary evaporator.
